summary: Gruber examines Apple's rushed but strategically brilliant entry into podcasting with iTunes 4.9, arguing that shipping early with bugs was the right call because it instantly made Apple the dominant player. He catalogs the shortcomings — broken ITMS feeds, missing UI options, iPod firmware regressions — but contends these are acceptable tradeoffs for speed to market. He notes the enormous branding windfall of the word 'podcasting' itself containing 'pod,' which forces competitors into impossible naming dilemmas. He also surveys the gaps in podcast creation tools and RSS standards compliance issues, concluding that Apple's willingness to fix problems after shipping is more important than getting everything right upfront.
key_points: Apple shipped podcasting support faster than expected for a company its size, accepting known bugs as the cost of beating competitors like Odeo to market
The word 'podcasting' is a permanent branding advantage for Apple, leaving competitors to choose between using a rival's name, coining an alternative nobody will adopt, or ignoring the trend entirely
Apple's podcasting strategy is a loss-leader: free iTunes and free podcast downloads drive iPod hardware sales, where the real profits are
iTunes's RSS parser quirks matter enormously because its dominance means 'works with iTunes' will become the de facto standard, much as IE defined HTML compatibility
Apple's lack of podcast creation tools is a minor gap — most people want to consume podcasts, not produce them — but it represents an opportunity for indie Mac developers
money_quotes: Real artists ship, and one of the main tricks to shipping software is knowing when to say, 'This is good enough.' Recognizing what qualifies as 'good enough' is art, not science.
All of a sudden, iTunes 4.9 has turned an entire class of software into an unnecessary middleman.
It's not because Odeo moved slowly; it's because Apple moved fast.
Apple has flipped the old Gillette maxim — they're making money selling the razors (iPods), not the blades.
Can you imagine Apple using a term like 'walkmancasting', 'dellcasting', or 'wincasting'? It's embarrassing.
If podcasting continues to grow and turns into something big, the simple fact that the name includes 'pod' is a significant and permanent advantage in Apple's favor.
It's easier to ask forgiveness than to seek permission — and, let's face it, Apple was not going to seek permission from anyone on something like this.
Apple is in a position to do whatever they want regarding podcasting, but for the most part, it appears that what they want to do is the right thing.
key_insight: Apple's decision to ship podcasting support early with known bugs was strategically correct because speed to market — combined with the branding windfall of the word 'podcasting' itself — created a dominant position that no amount of polish could have matched.
